@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Customizing Problem Details

articles.name: What Is Content Negotiation?
articles.summary:
When a client calls a Web API, it may want the response in different formats, such as **JSON** or **XML**.

**Content Negotiation** is the process of choosing the response format that both the client and the server support.

bodyBlocks.text:
## Imagine This Situation

Your API returns a city.

However, the controller returns a **C# object**, not JSON.

So how does the client receive JSON?

Something inside ASP.NET Core converts the C# object into a format that the client understands.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant Controller
participant "ASP.NET Core" as Api

Client -> Controller : GET /api/cities/1

Controller --> Api : CityDto

Api --> Client : JSON response

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: C# DTO Object to JSON Response

bodyBlocks.text:
## Different Clients Prefer Different Formats

Not every client application prefers the same response format.

For example:

- A web application usually expects **JSON**.
- Another system may expect **XML**.

Before sending the response, the client can tell the server which format it prefers.

## What Is Content Negotiation?

**Content Negotiation** is simply an agreement between the client and the server about the response format.

For example:

**Client**

--> I'd like the response in JSON.

**Server**

--> I support JSON, so I'll send JSON.

Or:

**Client**

--> I'd like the response in XML.

**Server**

--> I support XML, so I'll send XML.

If the requested format isn't supported, the server usually returns its default format, which is commonly **JSON**.

bodyBlocks.title: Content Negotiation

bodyBlocks.text:
## The Accept Header

The client tells the server which response format it prefers by using the **Accept** header.

For example:

- `Accept: application/json`

or

- `Accept: application/xml`

This header doesn't contain any data.

It only tells the server which format the client would like to receive.

## Output Formatter

Once ASP.NET Core knows which response format to use, an **Output Formatter** converts the C# object into that format.

For example, it can convert a `CityDto` into JSON or XML.

The controller doesn't create JSON or XML itself.

ASP.NET Core performs this conversion automatically.

## Input Formatter

When the client sends data to the server, the opposite happens.

The client sends JSON.

ASP.NET Core converts that JSON into a C# object.

Input formatters are commonly used for **POST**, **PUT**, and **PATCH** requests.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "Input Formatter" as Formatter
participant Controller

Client -> Formatter : JSON request body

Formatter -> Controller : CityDto

Controller --> Client : 201 Created

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Input Formatter

bodyBlocks.text:
## Accept vs Content-Type

These two HTTP headers are easy to confuse.

rows.text: Which response format the client wants
Which format the client is sending

bodyBlocks.text:
For example:

- `Accept: application/json`

means:

--> Please send the response as JSON.

Whereas:

- `Content-Type: application/json`

means:

--> I'm sending JSON to the server.

bodyBlocks.title: Accept Header
Content-Type Header
Accept Header

bodyBlocks.text:
## Does ASP.NET Core Do This Automatically?

Yes.

When you return:

- `return Ok(city);`

ASP.NET Core automatically:

- Performs Content Negotiation.
- Chooses an Output Formatter.
- Converts the object into the requested format.
- Sends the response to the client.

In most applications, you don't need to write any additional code.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
The **Accept** header tells which response format the client wants.

The **Content-Type** header tells which request format the client is sending.

ASP.NET Core automatically handles the conversion between C# objects and the requested format (**JSON**, **XML**).

articles.name: What Is 406 Not Acceptable?
articles.summary:
By default, ASP.NET Core returns its default response format, even if the client requests a format that isn't supported.

In this article, you'll learn how to enable **406 Not Acceptable** responses and make your API follow the client's requested response format more strictly.

bodyBlocks.text:
## The Default Behavior

Suppose your API only supports **JSON**.

The client sends the following request:

The client requests **PDF**, but the API can't generate PDF responses.

By default, ASP.NET Core ignores the unsupported format and returns its default response format instead, which is usually **JSON**.

## Returning the Default Format

Without any additional configuration, the response looks like this:
Even though the client requested **application/pdf**, the API still returns **JSON** because it is the default configured formatter.

## Enabling 406 Not Acceptable

ASP.NET Core can be configured to reject unsupported response formats.
When this option is enabled, ASP.NET Core follows the **Accept** header more strictly.

## What Changes?

Now imagine the same request:
Since the API doesn't support PDF responses, ASP.NET Core returns:
Instead of silently falling back to JSON, the API tells the client that it cannot produce the requested response format.

## When Is 406 Returned?

A **406 Not Acceptable** response is returned when:

- The client requests a response format using the **Accept** header.
- The API doesn't support that format.
- `ReturnHttpNotAcceptable` is enabled.

If the requested format is supported, the API returns the response normally.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
By default, ASP.NET Core returns its default response format when the requested format isn't supported.

Enabling `ReturnHttpNotAcceptable` changes this behavior and returns **406 Not Acceptable** instead.

bodyBlocks.title: Returning 406 Not Acceptable

articles.name: How ASP.NET Core Chooses a Formatter
articles.summary:
ASP.NET Core keeps **Input Formatters** and **Output Formatters** in an ordered list.

When processing a request or response, it checks the formatters from top to bottom and uses the **first formatter** that supports the requested media type.

bodyBlocks.text:
## Formatter Lists

ASP.NET Core stores **Input Formatters** and **Output Formatters** in an ordered list.

Each formatter knows how to read or write one or more data formats, such as **JSON** or **XML**.

When a request or response is processed, ASP.NET Core checks these formatters one by one until it finds a matching formatter.

## Why Does the Order Matter?

The order of the formatters is important.

If multiple formatters support the same media type, ASP.NET Core always uses the **first matching formatter** and ignores the rest.

For this reason, changing the order can change how your API processes requests and responses.

## Example

Imagine the following Output Formatter order:

1. JSON Formatter
2. XML Formatter
3. Custom Formatter

The client sends:

- `Accept: application/json`

ASP.NET Core checks the list from top to bottom.

The first formatter supports **JSON**, so it is selected immediately.

The remaining formatters are not checked.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

collections "Output Formatters" as Formatters

participant "ASP.NET Core" as Api

Client -> Api : Accept: application/json

Api -> Formatters : Check formatter #1

Formatters --> Api : JSON supported ✓

Api --> Client : JSON response

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Choosing an Output Formatter

bodyBlocks.text:
## Changing the Order

You can change the formatter order by inserting a formatter at the beginning of the list.

Now your custom formatter has the highest priority.

If it supports the requested media type, ASP.NET Core uses it before any other formatter.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
ASP.NET Core checks formatters **from top to bottom**.

The **first formatter** that supports the requested media type is selected to process the request or response.

articles.name: Adding XML Support
articles.summary:
By default, ASP.NET Core returns **JSON** responses.

You can also configure your API to support **XML** by adding an XML formatter.

bodyBlocks.text:
## JSON by Default

ASP.NET Core uses **JSON** as its default response format.

When you return a C# object from a controller, it is automatically converted into JSON.

## Adding XML Support

To support XML responses, register an XML formatter during application startup.
After adding the XML formatter, your API can return both **JSON** and **XML**.
Requesting XML Response

bodyBlocks.title: Requesting XML Response

bodyBlocks.text: Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
ASP.NET Core returns **JSON** by default.

By adding an XML formatter, the same API can also return **XML** without changing your controllers.

articles.name: Downloading a File from an API
articles.summary:
An ASP.NET Core Web API can return not only JSON but also files.

In this article, you'll learn how to read a file, detect its content type, and send it to the client for download.

bodyBlocks.text:
## Returning a File

An API can return many kinds of files, for example:

- PDF documents
- Images
- Excel files
- ZIP archives

ASP.NET Core provides the `File()` helper method to send files to the client.

## Check Whether the File Exists

Before returning a file, make sure it exists.

If it doesn't, return **404 Not Found**.

## Register the Content Type Provider

ASP.NET Core doesn't automatically know how to map every file extension to a **Content-Type**.

To detect file types, register a `FileExtensionContentTypeProvider` as a service.
Later, you can inject this service into your controller and use it to determine the correct **Content-Type** for a file.

## Detect the Content Type

The client needs to know what kind of file is being downloaded.

ASP.NET Core can detect the correct **Content-Type** from the file extension.
If the file type isn't recognized, ASP.NET Core uses the generic **application/octet-stream** type.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client
participant "ASP.NET Core Web API" as Api
database "File System" as Files
collections "Content Type Provider" as Provider

Client -> Api : GET /api/files/{id}

Api -> Files : Read file

Api -> Provider : Detect Content-Type

Provider --> Api : application/pdf

Api --> Client : PDF file

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Detecting the File Content Type

bodyBlocks.text:
## Return the File

Read the file and return it using the `File()` helper.
ASP.NET Core sends the file together with its **Content-Type** and filename.
Downloading a File

bodyBlocks.title: Downloading a File

bodyBlocks.text: Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
To return a file from an API:

1. Check that the file exists.
2. Detect its **Content-Type**.
3. Read the file.
4. Return it with the `File()` helper.

The client receives the file with the correct filename and media type.

articles.name: Why Do We Send Data to an API?
articles.summary: Some API requests only **read data**, while others **send data** to create, update, or delete resources. Understanding when data is sent is the first step toward building REST APIs.

bodyBlocks.text:
## Reading vs Changing Data

Not every API request sends data.

Some requests only **read information**, while others **change it**.

For example:

- **GET** retrieves data.
- **POST** creates new data.
- **PUT** replaces existing data.
- **PATCH** updates part of existing data.
- **DELETE** removes data.

When we create or update something, the API needs additional information. That's why we send data with the request.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml
left to right direction

rectangle "Client" as Client
rectangle "API" as Api

Client --> Api : GET\nRead data
Api --> Client : Response

Client --> Api : POST / PUT / PATCH\nSend data
Api --> Client : Resource created/updated

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Reading vs Changing Data

bodyBlocks.text:
## Example

Reading a city.

No additional data is required:

Creating a new city.

The API needs this information to create the new resource:

## Why This Matters

Whenever an API needs to **create**, **update**, or sometimes **search** using complex criteria, the client must send data along with the request.

In the next articles, you'll learn **where this data can be sent** and how **ASP.NET Core automatically reads it**.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
- **GET** usually reads data.
- **POST**, **PUT**, and **PATCH** send data to the API.
- The API uses that data to create or modify resources.
- ASP.NET Core can automatically read the data you send.

articles.name: Where Can Request Data Come From?
articles.summary: An HTTP request can send data in different places, such as the URL, query string, headers, or request body. ASP.NET Core can automatically read data from each of these sources.

bodyBlocks.text:
## Four Common Sources

When a client sends a request to an API, the data doesn't always come from the same place.

The four most common sources are:

- **Route** – part of the URL path.
- **Query string** – values after the `?` in the URL.
- **Headers** – request metadata.
- **Request body** – the main content of the request.

Each source is useful for different kinds of information.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Client

rectangle "HTTP Request" {
  rectangle "Route" as Route
  rectangle "Query String" as Query
  rectangle "Headers" as Headers
  rectangle "Request Body" as Body
}

rectangle "ASP.NET Core API" as Api

Client --> Route
Client --> Query
Client --> Headers
Client --> Body

Route --> Api
Query --> Api
Headers --> Api
Body --> Api

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Common Request Data Sources

bodyBlocks.text: Route
The value **5** is part of the URL.
Query String
The values come after the `?`.
Headers
Headers usually contain metadata, such as authentication tokens.
Request Body
The request body contains the data used to create or update a resource.

## How ASP.NET Core Uses Them

ASP.NET Core can automatically read values from these request sources and pass them to your controller action.

In the next articles, you'll learn how to read data from each source using features like **model binding** and attributes such as **`[FromRoute]`**, **`[FromQuery]`**, and **`[FromBody]`**.
Key Takeaway

- Request data can come from **Route**, **Query String**, **Headers**, or the **Request Body**.
- Each source is designed for a different purpose.
- ASP.NET Core can automatically read data from all of them.

articles.name: What Is Model Binding?
articles.summary:
Model Binding is an ASP.NET Core feature that automatically reads data from an HTTP request and passes it to your controller action.

Instead of manually reading request values, ASP.NET Core creates the method parameters for you.

bodyBlocks.text:
## The Problem

Imagine the client sends the following request.

The value **5** is part of the request.

But how does it become the `cityId` parameter in your controller action?
Someone has to read the request and assign the value to `cityId`.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "HTTP Request" as Request
participant "Controller" as Controller

Client -> Request : GET /api/cities/5

Request -> Controller : ?

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Request Before Model Binding

bodyBlocks.text:
## What Is Model Binding?

**Model Binding** is the process of reading data from an HTTP request and assigning it to your controller action parameters.

ASP.NET Core performs this automatically.

For example:
becomes:
without you writing any additional code.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "HTTP Request" as Request
participant "Model Binding" as Binding
participant "Controller" as Controller

Client -> Request : GET /api/cities/5

Request -> Binding : cityId = 5

Binding -> Controller : GetCity(5)

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Model Binding

bodyBlocks.text:
## It Works with More Than Route Values

Model Binding can read data from different parts of the request, including:

- Route
- Query String
- Headers
- Request Body

In the next articles, you'll learn how ASP.NET Core reads data from each of these sources.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

rectangle "HTTP Request" {

    rectangle Route
    rectangle "Query String" as Query
    rectangle Headers
    rectangle "Request Body" as Body
}

component "Model Binding"

rectangle Controller

Route --> "Model Binding"
Query --> "Model Binding"
Headers --> "Model Binding"
Body --> "Model Binding"

"Model Binding" --> Controller

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Model Binding Sources

bodyBlocks.text:
## Why Is It Useful?

Without Model Binding, every controller action would need to manually read values from the HTTP request.

Model Binding performs this work automatically, allowing you to focus on your application logic instead of parsing requests.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
Model Binding automatically reads data from an HTTP request and passes it to your controller action.

It works with data from the **Route**, **Query String**, **Headers**, and **Request Body**, so you don't have to read these values manually.

articles.name: Reading Route Values
articles.summary:
Route values are values that appear as part of the URL path.

In this article, you'll learn how ASP.NET Core automatically reads route values and passes them to your controller action.

bodyBlocks.text:
## What Is a Route Value?

A **route value** is a value that appears in the URL path.

For example:

In this request, the value **5** is a route value.

It identifies the city that the client wants to retrieve.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

participant "ASP.NET Core Web API" as Api

Client -> Api : GET /api/cities/5

note right of Api
Route value:
cityId = 5
end note

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Route Value

bodyBlocks.text:
## Reading the Route Value

ASP.NET Core automatically reads the route value and passes it to your controller action.
When the client requests:
the `cityId` parameter receives the value **5** automatically.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "HTTP Request" as Request
participant "Model Binding" as Binding
participant Controller

Client -> Request : GET /api/cities/5

Request -> Binding : cityId = 5

Binding -> Controller : GetCity(5)

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Model Binding Route Value

bodyBlocks.text:
## Using `[FromRoute]`

You can explicitly tell ASP.NET Core that a value should come from the route.

In most cases, this attribute isn't required because ASP.NET Core can determine it automatically.

However, using `[FromRoute]` can make your code more explicit and easier to read.

## When Should You Use It?

Use route values when the value identifies a specific resource.

Typical examples include:
Each route value identifies the resource that the client wants to access.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
A **route value** is part of the URL path.

ASP.NET Core automatically reads route values using **Model Binding** and passes them to your controller action.

The `[FromRoute]` attribute is optional in most cases, but it can make your code more explicit.

articles.name: Reading Query String Values
articles.summary:
Query string values are values that appear after the `?` in a URL.

In this article, you'll learn how ASP.NET Core automatically reads query string values and passes them to your controller action.

bodyBlocks.text: First heading
This is the first paragraph.

## What Is a Query String?

A **query string** is the part of a URL that appears after the `?`.

For example:

In this request:

- `page` has the value **2**
- `pageSize` has the value **10**

Query string values are commonly used for **filtering**, **sorting**, and **pagination**.

## Reading Query String Values

ASP.NET Core automatically reads query string values and passes them to your controller action.
When the client requests:
the `page` parameter receives **2** and the `pageSize` parameter receives **10** automatically.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "HTTP Request" as Request
participant "Model Binding" as Binding
participant Controller

Client -> Request : GET /api/cities?page=2&pageSize=10

Request -> Binding : page = 2\npageSize = 10

Binding -> Controller : GetCities(2, 10)

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Model Binding Query String Values

bodyBlocks.text:
## Using `[FromQuery]`

You can explicitly tell ASP.NET Core that a value should come from the query string.

In most cases, this attribute isn't required because ASP.NET Core can determine it automatically.

However, using `[FromQuery]` can make your code more explicit and easier to read.

## When Should You Use It?

Query string values are useful when the client needs to control **how data is returned**, rather than **which resource** is returned.

Typical examples include:
These values allow the client to customize the response without changing the resource itself.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
Query string values appear after the **`?`** in a URL.

ASP.NET Core automatically reads these values using **Model Binding** and passes them to your controller action.

The `[FromQuery]` attribute is optional in most cases, but it can make your code more explicit.

articles.name: Reading Header Values
articles.summary:
HTTP headers contain additional information about a request, such as authentication tokens, language preferences, or custom metadata.

In this article, you'll learn how ASP.NET Core reads header values and passes them to your controller action.

bodyBlocks.text:
## What Is a Header?

An **HTTP header** contains additional information about a request.

Unlike route values or query string values, headers are **not part of the URL**. They are sent separately with every HTTP request.

Common examples include:

- `Authorization`
- `User-Agent`
- `Accept`
- `Content-Type`

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client
participant "ASP.NET Core Web API" as Api

Client -> Api : HTTP Request

note right of Api
Authorization
Accept
Content-Type
User-Agent
end note

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: HTTP Request Headers

bodyBlocks.text:
## Reading Header Values

ASP.NET Core can automatically read a header value and pass it to your controller action.
If the request contains the following header:
the `userAgent` parameter receives the value **"Mozilla/5.0"** automatically.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml

actor Client

participant "HTTP Request" as Request
participant "Model Binding" as Binding
participant Controller

Client -> Request : HTTP GET

Request -> Binding : User-Agent = Mozilla/5.0

Binding -> Controller : GetData(userAgent)

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Model Binding Header Values

bodyBlocks.text:
## Common Uses

Headers are often used to send information that applies to the entire request instead of a specific resource.

Typical examples include:

- Authentication tokens
- Preferred language
- Client application information
- Correlation or request IDs

## When Should You Use It?

Use headers when the information describes the **request itself**, not the resource being accessed.

For example:

- Authentication → `Authorization`
- Preferred response format → `Accept`
- Language preference → `Accept-Language`
- Request tracking → `X-Correlation-ID`
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
Headers contain additional information about an HTTP request.

ASP.NET Core automatically reads header values using **Model Binding** and passes them to your controller action.

Use the **`[FromHeader]`** attribute when you want to explicitly read a value from a request header.

articles.name: Reading Request Body
articles.summary: Learn how ASP.NET Core reads data sent in the HTTP request body using the `[FromBody]` attribute.

bodyBlocks.text:
When creating or updating resources, the client usually sends data in the **request body**.

For example, a client might send:

ASP.NET Core can automatically read this JSON and convert it into a C# object. This process is called **model binding**.

For API requests, the data usually comes from the request body, so we use the **`[FromBody]`** attribute.

ASP.NET Core:

- Reads the request body.
- Converts the JSON into a `CityDto` object.
- Passes the object to the action method.

The client must also send the correct **Content-Type** header:
Without it, ASP.NET Core may not know how to read the request body.
Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary: Use **`[FromBody]`** when the client sends data in the **HTTP request body**, most commonly as JSON.

bodyBlocks.title: Reading Request Body

articles.name: What Is a DTO?
articles.summary: A **DTO (Data Transfer Object)** is a simple object used to transfer data between a client and an API. It contains only the data that should be sent or received.

bodyBlocks.text:
A **DTO (Data Transfer Object)** is a simple C# class or record whose only purpose is to **transfer data**.

It is commonly used to:

- Receive data from a client.
- Send data back to a client.

A DTO should contain **data only**. It should not contain business logic or database operations.
Example DTO

## Why Use DTOs?

Instead of exposing your internal application classes, you expose **DTOs**.

This lets you:

- Hide internal implementation details.
- Return only the data clients need.
- Keep your API stable even if your application changes.

## How a DTO Is Used

When creating a resource:

1. The client sends JSON.
2. ASP.NET Core converts it into a DTO.
3. Your controller uses the DTO.
4. The API returns another DTO in the response.

bodyBlocks.diagram:
@startuml
' title Using a DTO

actor Client
participant API
entity "CityDto" as DTO

Client -> API : Send JSON
API -> DTO : Convert JSON
DTO --> API : Data
API --> Client : Return DTO

@enduml
bodyBlocks.title: Using a DTO

bodyBlocks.text: Key Takeaway

bodyBlocks.summary:
A **DTO (Data Transfer Object)** is a simple object used to **transfer data** between a client and an API.

It helps keep your API clean, secure, and independent from internal application models.
